serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Navratan Korma is approximately 300 g, representing one main-course portion served with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable curry, with coconut milk / cream-based mixed vegetable curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central reference values for mixed vegetable korma components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Navratan Korma exists. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entries and USDA FoodData Central data for vegetable korma-type dishes (cream and nut-based sauce, mixed vegetables, paneer optional). Energy and macros cross-referenced against published Indian restaurant food composition studies. Values represent a moderate-richness restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry and legume-vegetable dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Navratan Korma. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous mixed vegetable curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Mixed vegetable curries with cream/nut-based sauces have low GI due to fat and protein content slowing gastric emptying, and the predominance of low-GI vegetables. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 31.5) / 100 ≈ 14.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard restaurant serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 300 g serving contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 42% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~14 per serving) and moderate carbohydrate content with fat-attenuated glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Navratan Korma has a low glycemic index (~45) due to its fat and protein content, but the glycemic load per restaurant serving is moderate (~14); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with a low-GI grain such as basmati rice and monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream, ghee, and cashews (~3.2 g per 100 g; ~9.6 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A full restaurant serving of Navratan Korma provides approximately 9.6 g of saturated fat, approaching the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions and request preparation with reduced ghee and cream.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Navratan Korma, also known as Subji Navratan or Nine Jewels Vegetable Curry, is a rich North Indian Mughal-origin vegetable dish in which nine types of vegetables, nuts, and sometimes paneer are cooked in a mildly spiced, cream- and cashew-based sauce. The name 'navratan' (Sanskrit: nine jewels) refers to the nine key ingredients traditionally used, which typically include carrots, peas, green beans, cauliflower, potatoes, corn, capsicum, paneer, and cashew nuts, with optional dried fruits such as raisins. The dish is prepared by first making a smooth onion-tomato-cashew gravy, seasoning it with whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon) and ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala), then simmering the vegetables and paneer in the sauce before finishing with cream or yogurt. The result is a mildly sweet, aromatic, and creamy curry with a velvety texture and jewel-like colours from the varied vegetables. Navratan Korma is vegetarian and is commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, a 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 354 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 22.5 g fat, and 31.5 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (~45) due to its fat and protein content, but a moderate glycemic load (~14) per serving. Sodium content is significant at approximately 960 mg per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The saturated fat content from cream and ghee (~9.6 g per serving) is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is rich in beta-carotene, vitamin C, and calcium from its diverse vegetable and dairy components.

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore entry for Navratan Korma exists. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data and USDA FoodData Central reference values for cream and nut-based vegetable curry preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar mixed vegetable curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and cross-referenced against multiple approved sources.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA reference sources for this dish type. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary reference sources for mixed vegetable curry preparations. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from approved sources. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Navratan Korma has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. The restaurant serving this dish at Maharani Table - Boat Quay (field survey data) may use a proprietary recipe; actual nutrient values may vary. The dish is classified as Indian cuisine served in Singapore context. Navratan Korma is inherently vegetarian but not vegan due to dairy (cream, ghee, paneer). Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian and dairy-only composition, but restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified.
